story:
A plain concrete motorway bridge spans the M25, high over a deep, man-made valley of rushing lanes. It is not grand like the QEII over the Thames, nor one of those flat, forgettable crossings. It has a simple elegance: one great arch and a straight line across the top, a curve and a line. Only when you rush beneath it and glance up, feeling as if you’re standing still, do you sense its size. I used to call it my bridge because it marked a boundary in me—the line between the known and the unknown. Coming home from long trips, it was the first certain sign I was about forty minutes from home. On the way out, a huge road sign just before the bridge forks the motorway into choices. Pass under the arch, and you leave the known behind for routes you can name but not fully predict—new pleasures, new sadness, all to be discovered.

Today we are heading toward the bridge, away from home. I’m driving, my wife beside me. In the rearview mirror sit my daughter, radiant in her wedding dress, and her new husband. The gown is long and silky, the kind of beauty all her childhood pretending—a princess, a ballerina—had been rehearsing for. Her smile echoes her mother’s on our own day, and there’s a sparkle in her eyes that takes me back.

Once, when she was five, we were out for a day trip, playing “spot.” I knew the bridge was coming and said, “See if you can spot a bridge.” When it rose into view, she lit up. “Daddy, it’s a rainbow.” Later we found a wishing well. “Can we make a wish?” she asked, and dropped her penny. “You mustn’t tell or it won’t come true.” I didn’t believe, not really, but I played along. It was a hot, happy day. Driving home, rain started, traffic thickened, then the sun broke through as the bridge came into sight. “Look, Daddy—there’s a rainbow. That’s what I wished for.” I couldn’t tell if she meant the sky or the arch. I didn’t look up; the road was wet. Either way, her wish had been granted. Passing under what I now call Rainbow Bridge, I made a wish of my own: that she would always be as happy as she was that day, that the world would keep giving her simple, shining pleasures, and I knew we’d pass under it again with her in her wedding dress behind me.

Nearly twenty years later, I think it has. She was happy today. There’s no prouder moment for a father than giving his little girl away. As we drive them to the airport for their honeymoon, I feel the gentle sadness of parting, even if it’s only weeks. The forked sign appears; our roads are branching. I slip on my sunglasses though the light is soft. We pass under Rainbow Bridge. I silently thank whatever power has kept her happy this long and ask the magic to go with her. We leave the known and enter the unknown—different directions, undiscovered joys and sorrows—something to savor, and something to look forward to.
